cataloging_subject.label: An Alphabet of Tales
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The anonymous fifteenth-century Middle English translation of Arnold of Liege's Latin Alphabetum narrationum, preserved complete in British Library Add MS 25719. This subject excludes Arnold's Latin compilation, the physical manuscript, Mary Macleod Banks's 1904-1905 two-volume edition, the U-M electronic encoding, and the repository XML artifact.

catalog_summary.title: An Alphabet of Tales
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous translator (from Arnold of Liege)
catalog_summary.region: Northern England; the Ribble Valley and Salley (Sawley) Abbey are a later scholarly proposal

notes: This is the uniform title in the U-M header, not a medieval manuscript title.

titles.value: An Alphabet of Tales: An English 15th Century Translation of the Alphabetum Narrationum Once Attributed to Etienne de Besancon, from Additional MS. 25,719 of the British Museum

scope.description: Banks's revised title wording on the 1905 Part II title page

notes: The 1904 Part I title page still names Etienne de Besancon without the qualification 'once attributed to'.

titles.value: Abbas non debet esse nimis rigidus. Vnde Anselmus. Som tyme ther was ane abbott...
notes: The opening Latin rubric is followed by the Middle English tale; the Latin source prologue is absent from the English witness.

agents.name: Mary Macleod Banks
notes: Editor of the two-volume EETS edition published in 1904 and 1905.

scope.description: Middle English translation of Arnold of Liege's Latin Alphabetum narrationum

notes: Neither the translator nor the exact Latin exemplar used for the translation has been identified in the sources reviewed.

scope.description: Banks's 1904-1905 scholarly transcription and apparatus, the immediate source edition for the U-M encoding
Comparison of difficult or confused English readings against Latin copies in Harley MS 268 and Arundel MS 378

notes: The two Latin manuscripts are Banks's comparison witnesses; this does not establish either as the anonymous medieval translator's exemplar.

assertion.notes: The surviving manuscript is fifteenth-century and Herbert dates it to about the middle of the century. EETS conventionally advertised the translation as about 1440, while later linguistic literature often uses c. 1450; no exact translation date was established.
Banks published the complete translation in two EETS parts, A-H in 1904 and I-Z in 1905.

places.label: Ribble Valley
notes: Miura (following Johnson) places the translation at Salley Abbey, now usually called Sawley Abbey, in the Ribble area where Lancashire adjoins Yorkshire; this is a scholarly proposal, not a manuscript colophon.

assertion.notes: Miura reports the mid-fifteenth-century Salley (Sawley) Abbey/Ribble-area proposal from Johnson. The proposal is useful but remains indirect here because Johnson's full restricted dissertation was not inspected.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: The translation is in a Northern Middle English variety.

language_statements.label: Latin

scope.description: Latin thematic rubrics, source labels, quotations, cross-references, and the scribal colophon

notes: The XML marks 1,062 FOREIGN elements as Latin; that is an encoding metric, not a count of distinct intellectual parts.

language_statements.label: French

scope.description: One short French quotation encoded within a tale

notes: The XML marks one FOREIGN element as French; French is not a co-primary language of the translation.

form_statements.description: An alphabetically organized prose collection; brief quotations and the four-line Latin scribal colophon do not change the whole-work form.

summaries.value: An anonymous Northern Middle English translation of Arnold of Liege's Alphabetum narrationum, an alphabetically indexed repertory of short moral and religious narratives intended especially for preaching and instruction. The English witness lacks the Latin prologue but preserves Latin subject rubrics, cross-references, and source labels around more than eight hundred prose tale divisions.

The source edition's attribution to Etienne de Besancon is historical. Banks's 1905 second title page already says 'once attributed to', and current specialist resources identify Arnold of Liege as compiler of the Latin source. Arnold is not the identified author or translator of the anonymous Middle English expression.

extent.description: Encoding metric: 802 numbered tale DIV1 units, comprising Roman numerals I-DCCCI plus an inserted LXVIIIa. The XML has 806 DIV1 elements in all: two editorial notes, 803 chapter-typed divisions (the 802 tales and a separate Explicit division), and one errata division. The four-line Latin scribal colophon in the Explicit division is excluded from work-form and verse totals.

content_parts.label: Alphabetically arranged tales
content_parts.summary: The complete English translation runs from the subject Abbas through Ypocrisis and is organized by Latin alphabetical headings, individual tale rubrics, and cross-references.
notes: Banks's two editorial notes, the Explicit division, and the errata are edition or witness matter rather than separate intellectual tale parts.

related_works.title: Alphabetum narrationum
notes: The Latin collection is now attributed to Arnold of Liege and dated 1297-1308. Etienne de Besancon is a rejected historical attribution retained in the source edition's bibliographic title.

evidence.citation: General Corpus, CME/source/CME_phase_1-2/AlphTales.xml, header, editorial declaration, notes, full text, explicit, and errata.
evidence.summary: Supplies the U-M identifier and 1997 publication statement, Banks source edition, historical attribution warning, encoding production and consolidation practices, full English text, embedded Latin and French markup, and edition pagination.

evidence.locator: DIV1, HEAD, PB, and FOREIGN elements
evidence.summary: Counts 806 DIV1 elements, 802 numbered tales, 532 page milestones, 1,062 Latin FOREIGN elements, and one French FOREIGN element; detects the inserted LXVIIIa and the edition's duplicated CLXXI/missing CLXXII numbering.

evidence.citation: Mary Macleod Banks, ed., An Alphabet of Tales, EETS Original Series 126-127 (London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner & Co., 1904-1905), 2 parts.
evidence.locator: Part I title page and Note; Part II title page and Note; pp. 1-532 and errata
evidence.summary: The inspected complete scans establish the two-part edition, direct Add MS 25719 source, A-H and I-Z division, editorial restoration, supply, capitalization and punctuation conventions, use of Harley 268 and Arundel 378 for difficult readings, 532-page text, and the 1905 rejection of Etienne de Besancon's authorship.
notes: Part I SHA-256: a9a3038f629c388cce80aae59e42cab2aa75650e24ba2d0e9935e8cdaafcd00b. Part II SHA-256: ec6138c76141ebf6693bc7527c6707b0e62a1ff5781c6692e07a3758fd5e8cba.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Add MS 25719, record 032-002030060.
evidence.locator: Title, scope and content, extent, language, date range, and access
evidence.summary: Identifies the English translation witness, current shelfmark, paper support, missing preface, Latin rhyming colophon, fifteenth-century date, English language, final service-book flyleaf at f. 185, and Reading Room request route; the Digitised Content field is blank.

evidence.citation: J. A. Herbert, 'The Authorship of the Alphabetum Narrationum,' The Library, 2nd ser., 6.21 (1905): 94-101.
evidence.summary: Rejects the attribution to Etienne de Besancon, adduces the post-1297 and 1308 evidence, reads the prologue acrostic as Arnuldus, and argues for Arnold of Liege; Banks cites this article in Part II.

evidence.citation: J. A. Herbert, Catalogue of Romances in the Department of Manuscripts in the British Museum, vol. 3 (London: British Museum, 1910).
evidence.summary: Describes the Latin Alphabetum and comparison witnesses, then Add MS 25719 as a mid-fifteenth-century paper manuscript of 184 folios, a Northumbrian English translation lacking the prologue, with four Latin tales omitted and a Latin scribal colophon.

evidence.locator: Bibliography, manuscript, edition, date, language, genre, form, incipit, and explicit
evidence.summary: Treats the English work as a fifteenth-century prose exemplum translation of Arnold of Liege's Alphabetum, identifies Add MS 25719 as its witness, and records Banks's two-volume 1904-1905 edition.

evidence.citation: Universidad Complutense de Madrid, MANIPULUS, 'Alphabetum narrationum (1297-1308),' handle 20.500.12238/43.
evidence.summary: Identifies Arnoldus Leodiensis as author of the Latin Alphabetum narrationum, dates the compilation 1297-1308, classifies it as exempla, and cites the 2015 Brilli critical edition and extensive manuscript tradition.

evidence.citation: Ayumi Miura, 'Reception of Latin Vocabulary and Introduction of New Words in An Alphabet of Tales,' Language and Culture Collaborative Research Project 2022 (2023): 20-33.
evidence.summary: Describes An Alphabet of Tales as an anonymous mid-fifteenth-century Northern English translation surviving uniquely in Add MS 25719, reports the Salley Abbey/Ribble-area provenance proposal from Johnson, identifies Banks as the only complete edition, and notes the unfulfilled Part III plan.

open_questions.question: Can the date and place of the Middle English translation be established more securely than the conventional c. 1440-c. 1450 Northern dating and the Salley Abbey/Ribble-area proposal reported by Miura from Johnson?
next_steps: Consult Johnson's full dissertation through lawful access and compare its linguistic dataset with current LALME or LAEME localization.
Inspect Add MS 25719 or a complete facsimile for codicological evidence distinguishing translation, exemplar, and copying dates.

open_questions.question: Who translated An Alphabet of Tales, and which Latin manuscript or textual branch supplied the translation?
next_steps: Consult the 2015 Brilli edition and specialist transmission studies for an English-version stemmatic analysis.
Do not treat Banks's comparison copies Harley MS 268 and Arundel MS 378 as the translator's exemplars without direct evidence.

notes: The historical source-edition spelling 'Etienne de Besancon' is retained without diacritics in supplied English metadata for interoperability; the edition scans use Etienne de Besancon and the XML uses Etienne de Besancon in normalized bibliographic data.
The repository XML's language header lists only Latin and French because Middle English is the unmarked base language of the text.
